← the Atlas

Graph view + Maps of Content

feature · seedling ·proposed ·JUN 19, 2026

Render the Atlas as a force-directed graph and surface its hub notes as Maps of Content — both derived from the links the Atlas already has, baked to a self-contained page at build time.

Contents

  • It reuses the links the Atlas already has
  • How the page stays a self-contained file
  • Where this could go

Spike. A second view of the same Atlas: a force-directed graph of every note, plus a Maps of Content strip that names the hubs — the notes the rest of the Atlas points at. The index answers “what’s filed where”; the graph answers “what’s connected to what,” and the hubs answer “where do I start.”

Take a look: open the graph. Hover a node to trace its neighborhood, click to open the note, drag/scroll to move around.

It reuses the links the Atlas already has

No new frontmatter, no hand-curated index. The edges are exactly the two the Atlas already authors, and the same derivation that powers the dead-link gate and the “Referenced by” backlinks now also feeds the graph:

  • parents — the within-category nesting edges.
  • same-directory prose links — every ./slug.html reference one note makes to another.

A node’s degree (how many edges touch it) sets its size; its inbound count (how many notes point at it) ranks it as a hub. The real corpus already concentrates: electricity is the gravitational center, with solid-fileview, pty-daemon, surface-app, and odu as the domain hubs. Those are the Maps of Content — discovered, not declared.

How the page stays a self-contained file

The Atlas’s whole premise is that each page is a committed, self-contained HTML file that previews in kolu’s Code tab with no dev server — and the build is a pure function of source (the ci::atlas-sync idempotency gate rebuilds under a scrambled timezone/locale and fails on any drift). The graph honors both:

  • Layout is computed at build time with d3-force (a library, not hand-rolled) and baked into a static inline <svg> as rounded coordinates. Nothing lays out at runtime.
  • Determinism is engineered in: notes are sorted by id and edges by pair-key before the simulation, positions are seeded by that stable index, a fixed number of ticks runs synchronously, and every coordinate is rounded. d3-force v3 carries no Math.random, so the baked geometry is identical on every rebuild.
  • The only client JS is one is:inline script for interaction (zoom/pan, hover-highlight, click-to-open) — so no _astro bundle is emitted and the page stays one file. Nodes are real <a> links, and the Maps-of-Content cards are plain linked lists, so the page still reads and navigates with JavaScript off.

Why a graph, given the index already exists

The generated index is the authoritative, scannable table of contents — it never goes away. The graph is a complementary lens: it makes the shape of the knowledge base legible (clusters, bridges, orphans) in a way a tree can’t, and the Maps of Content give a high-degree note a reason to exist as a deliberate entry point. The two share one data source, so they can never disagree.

Where this could go

  • ✓ spikeBuild-time d3-force layout, baked self-contained SVG, MoC hub cards, linked from the index.
  • ○ 1Filter/focus by kind or maturity; isolate a single hub’s cluster.
  • ○ 2Surface orphans (degree 0) and weakly-linked notes as a “wire these up” nudge.
  • ○ 3Deep-link into the graph centered on the note you came from (a “see this in context” link from each note).

Seedling spike — a second view over the existing link graph. The index stays the source of truth; this is a way to see it.
